relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Heaven’s reign is like a vineyard owner seeking workers.
The first agree to the day’s wage.
Others are found waiting in the marketplace.
They are invited into the vineyard with trust in justice.
The invitation repeats through the day.
Even at the eleventh hour, the idle are seen.
No one had hired them, so they too are sent.
Payment begins with the last.
The last receive a full denarius.
The first expect more but receive what was agreed.
Comparison turns gift into grievance.
They resent equality with the latecomers.
The owner has done no injustice.
Generosity toward the last is his will.
A harmful eye cannot rejoice in goodness.
The last-first reversal stands.
Jesus goes up toward Jerusalem with the twelve.
He names the handing-over and condemnation ahead.
The path goes through mockery, crucifixion, and third-day rising.
A mother asks for places of honor for her sons.
She seeks right and left seats in the reign.
Jesus asks whether they can drink his cup.
The cup is theirs, but the seats belong to the Father’s preparation.
The ten are indignant at the two.
Jesus contrasts nation-power with his way.
Greatness among disciples becomes service.
Firstness becomes slavery to love.
The Son of Humanity serves and gives his life as ransom for many.
They leave Jericho with a crowd following.
Two blind men cry for mercy from the Son of David.
The crowd tries to silence them, but mercy-cries grow louder.
Jesus stops and asks what they desire.
They ask for opened eyes.
Compassion touches their eyes, sight returns, and they follow.
